title: Sojourners and Pilgrims
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: 11-12-23 -AM- Sojourners and Pilgrims - 1 Peter 2_11-12 - Pastor Tito Lyro.txt

outline.title: The New Birth and the Remaining Flesh
subpoints: Christians have been born again to a living hope through the Spirit
They are citizens of a heavenly country, called out of darkness into light
Yet a remnant of the old self remains — what Peter calls 'fleshly lusts'
Sanctification: progressively becoming what we have been declared to be
The normal Christian experience is warfare between flesh and spirit (Romans 7)

outline.title: What Are Fleshly Lusts?
subpoints: Galatians 5:19-21 gives a representative (not exhaustive) list
Even good desires that dominate the heart become fleshly (James 4:1-3)
We are creative in our sinning — no list is complete
The seed of every kind of sin dwells in our fallen nature

outline.title: Two Motivations to Abstain
subpoints: Love: Peter addresses them as 'beloved' — not a killjoy, but acting as love acts
Identity: we are sojourners and pilgrims, foreigners passing through
Israel in the wilderness provides the imagery — not drawing roots, harassed by locals
Our citizenship is in heaven (Philippians 3:20)
No point in acting as citizens of a world we do not belong to

outline.title: How We Abstain: Honorable Conduct (1 Peter 2:12)
subpoints: We abstain from fleshly lusts by having our conduct honorable among unbelievers
Not just stopping bad things but putting on the positive things of Christ
Christians have been accused throughout history: cannibalism, atheism, incest
Yet consistent holy living leads unbelievers to glorify God on the day of their salvation
The rest of 1 Peter (2:13 through 5:11) describes what honorable conduct looks like
It is the stuff of everyday life: submit to authority, love one another, suffer well

summary: Pastor Tito Lyro resumes the Sunday morning series in 1 Peter, preaching on 1 Peter 2:11-12. He begins by establishing the context: the Christian has been born again to a living hope and is a citizen of heaven, called out of darkness into God's marvelous light. Yet because of the remaining influence of the old nature — what Peter calls 'fleshly lusts' — there is a constant inner warfare. Drawing on Galatians 5:19-21 and James 4:1-3, he shows that fleshly desires are not always obviously wicked — even good desires that dominate the heart become inordinate. Peter gives two motivations to abstain: love for his readers, and their identity as sojourners and pilgrims who do not belong to this world. The how of abstaining is given in verse 12 — having one's conduct honorable among unbelievers. Rather than merely stopping sinful behaviors, Christians are to put on positive, God-glorifying conduct that, like water dripping on a rock, eventually leads those around them to glorify God on the day He visits them with salvation. The sermon introduces verses 13-5:11 as the detailed unfolding of what such honorable conduct looks like in every sphere of everyday life.
transcript:
## Scripture Reading: 1 Peter 2:11-17

If you're able to remain standing for the reading of God's Word, this morning we're reading from 1 Peter chapter 2, verses 11 through 17. The second chapter of the first epistle of Peter.

"Beloved, I beg you as sojourners and pilgrims abstain from fleshly lusts, which war against the soul, having your conduct honorable among the Gentiles, that when they speak against you as evildoers, they may by your good works which they observe glorify God in the day of visitation. Therefore submit yourselves to every ordinance of men for the Lord's sake, whether to the King as supreme or to governors as to those who are sent by him for the punishment of evildoers and for the praise of those who do good. For this is the will of God, that by doing good you may put to silence the ignorance of foolish men. As free, yet not using liberty as a cloak for vice, but as bondservants of God, honor all people, love the brotherhood, fear God, honor the King."

This is the Word of our Lord. Let us pray together. Father, as your word is proclaimed this morning we pray that you encourage the fainthearted and strengthen the weak. We pray that your word would have its way with us. In Jesus' name, amen. Please be seated.

## The Christian's Identity and the Inner War

The Christian has been born again by the Spirit of God unto a living hope. We've seen in previous sermons that the Spirit of God has worked in us, given us a new heart and a new birth, so that we now live in hope of the return of the Lord Jesus Christ. As Peter says, the Christian has been called out of darkness into the marvelous light of God in the Lord Jesus Christ. You and I, by virtue of our faith in the Lord Jesus Christ, are citizens of a heavenly country and members of the race of Christ — the race of the second Adam, the Lord Jesus Christ.

And it is to us that the Holy Spirit through the Apostle Peter says: "Beloved, I beg you as sojourners and pilgrims abstain from fleshly lusts which war against the soul, having your conduct honorable among the Gentiles."

So the Holy Spirit through the Apostle Peter tells us: abstain from the desires of the flesh in verse 11. The word abstain is not difficult to understand — keep your distance, don't be involved with, stay away from, don't give in to. Peter tells us not to give into the desires that are left over from our old heart.

We are born again. We are given a new heart that is able to believe and by God's grace able to obey. That was a core promise of the new covenant announced in the Old Testament. In Ezekiel 36:26-27, God says: "I will give you a new heart and put a new spirit within you. I will take the heart of stone out of your flesh and give you a heart of flesh. I will put my spirit within you and cause you to walk in my statutes."

The Apostle Paul in 2 Corinthians 5:17 says: "If anyone is in Christ, he is a new creation. Old things have passed away, behold all things have become new."

So after being born again and coming to faith in Jesus, you are a new person. But there is still a remnant of the influence of your old heart, your old nature. This is true whether you were born again at age 50 or in the womb. There is a leftover from the old self that is progressively being killed. That is the doctrine of sanctification — progressively becoming more and more like the person we have been declared to be in Jesus Christ.

And this remnant — this leftover from who we were without Christ — is what Peter calls "fleshly lusts" in verse 11. The flesh is the aspect of us still in rebellion against God. And these desires are at war with who we are in Christ. That is what Paul describes in Romans 7 as the representative Christian: "The things that I know I should do, I end up not doing them; the things I know I shouldn't do, I end up doing those."

That is the normal experience of the Christian. When we forget about this constant inner warfare, we inevitably give in to the fleshly lusts Peter is talking about.

## What Are Fleshly Lusts?

This exhortation to abstain tells us at least two things: first, all of us must be aware that we are capable of falling into fleshly desires. The seed of every kind of sin dwells already in our fallen nature. Second, it tells us that we can successfully battle these desires — the exhortation to abstain implies we can do it by the grace of God.

Matthew Henry wrote: "Even the best of men, the chosen generation, the people of God, need an exhortation to abstain from the worst of sins."

So what are these fleshly lusts? We cannot exhaust the list — we are very creative in our sinning. But the Bible gives representative lists. In Galatians 5:19-21, Paul gives a list of seventeen fleshly desires and ends brilliantly with: "and the like" — as if to say, and anything that resembles these is also on the list. The list includes: adultery, fornication, uncleanness, lewdness, idolatry, sorcery, hatred, contentions, jealousies, outbursts of wrath, selfish ambitions, dissensions, divisions, envy, murders, drunkenness, revelries.

In 1 John 2:16 John speaks of the lust of the flesh, the lust of the eyes, and the pride of life. In James 4:1-3, James notes that our conflicts in life often spring from desires that rule our hearts — and he is not even speaking of obviously bad desires, but good desires that have taken the throne of the heart and become inordinate. A desire for your children to follow the Lord, for example, or for a peaceful marriage — these are good things that can dominate the heart and become fleshly.

## Two Motivations to Abstain

Peter gives us two motivations to abstain from fleshly lusts.

The first is love. Notice how he addresses them in verse 11: "Beloved" — my loved ones. Peter is not a killjoy saying "I want to tell you not to enjoy anything." People sometimes say a Puritan is a person worried that someone somewhere is having fun. But that caricature could not be further from the truth — the Puritans were people full of joy. Peter is acting here the way love is supposed to act. He loves these people he is writing to, just as 1 Corinthians 13:6 says love does not rejoice in iniquity but rejoices in truth.

The second motivation is identity — who we are in Christ. We are sojourners and pilgrims. We are foreigners. I have lived in the United States for over thirty years; I am still a foreigner to many people. There is always this sense of not quite belonging. That is exactly our identity in Christ in this world. We are foreigners, sojourners, pilgrims. Peter brings up the imagery of the wilderness wandering of Israel — they were going somewhere else. They were not interested in drawing roots in the desert. And that is our identity: we don't belong to this world, we are on our way to heaven, citizens and representatives of the kingdom of God.

Paul says in Philippians 3:20: "Our citizenship is in heaven, from which we also eagerly wait for the Savior, the Lord Jesus Christ." And Jesus himself said: "My kingdom is not of this world."

We are in the world — we need the world to get to places, just as a ship needs water. But once the ship is taking water, the water is deadly. That is the idea of being in the world.

## How We Abstain: Honorable Conduct

Peter begs us — the strongest possible word — to abstain from fleshly lusts. And he may seem to tell us to abstain without telling us how. But verse 12 gives us the how: "having your conduct honorable among the Gentiles."

The word "having" with the -ing ending is explaining how we abstain. We abstain from fleshly lusts by having our conduct honorable among unbelievers. We abstain from them by living according to the Word of God, by the grace of God.

God is not merely interested in us stopping bad behaviors. He wants us to put off what is inconsistent with who we are in Christ and to put on the very things that demonstrate that we are followers of Christ. Remember the story Jesus tells about the man whose demon is cast out, his house swept clean, but no other spirit takes up residence — so the demon comes back and brings more. Jesus is about putting off the old and putting on the new.

So the Holy Spirit says here that we are to live our lives — the way we conduct ourselves among unbelievers — in a way that brings honor to Jesus. As we do that, verse 12 says we will be spoken of as evildoers. Christians throughout history have been accused of cannibalism (because of how they spoke of eating Christ's body and drinking His blood), of incest (because they married their brothers and sisters in Christ), and of atheism (because they refused to worship all the gods). As we endeavor to live consistently with who we are in Christ, having our conduct honorable, they will attack us.

But as unbelievers see us constantly glorifying God in our lives — that is what good works do — they will eventually glorify God themselves, likely through their own salvation. This is what verse 12 calls "the day of visitation" — the day God visits the unbeliever and makes him able to glorify God by saving him.

There is a Portuguese saying: "Even soft water, as it drips consistently on a rock without changing, eventually makes a hole in that rock." That is the idea of holy living. We live holy, we are attacked — but as those around us see that consistent living for the glory of God, they will come to the Lord Jesus Christ.

Gladys Aylward is an example of this. She failed a missionary board's IQ test and so went to China on her own. She was not necessarily impressive by the world's standards. But by living daily life with the Chinese people, many came to faith in the Lord Jesus Christ. Not glorious things you read about in the news — just living life daily for the glory of God.

These two verses (11-12) introduce the rest of the book — all the way to chapter 5 verse 11 — which describes what honorable conduct looks like in every area of everyday life:

- In verse 13: submitting to governing authorities
- In verse 18: slaves submitting to masters
- In chapter 3:1: wives submitting to husbands
- In chapter 3:7: husbands dwelling with their wives in understanding
- In chapter 3:8: having compassion for one another
- In chapter 4:10: living out the gifts God has given us in ministry
- In chapter 5:6: humbling ourselves
- In chapter 5:8: fighting the devil

This is the stuff of every day life. The greatest detriment to the gospel is not Satan. It's not the world. The greatest detriment to the gospel is when you and I as followers of Jesus Christ are not living lives consistent with who we are in Jesus Christ.

Brothers and sisters, let us abstain from sinful desires which wage war against our soul by living such good lives among the pagans that, though they accuse us of doing wrong, they may see our good deeds and glorify God on the day He visits them with salvation — because we have pointed them to Christ by how we live and by how we speak.

Let's pray together. Father, thank you that you are God who works mightily through your people. We pray that we will be faithful to you and that we would not be a discouragement to people coming to Jesus Christ. But as we live lives that glorify you, people would see that and want to know who this Jesus is that so dominates our lives. Give us that hope and give us the ability to explain the reason for that hope. In Jesus' name, amen.
